sentences.text: We were staying in Biesenthal for about six weeks or maybe two months.
Berlin was already at that time taken over by the Russian army and by the Polish army.
And we ourselves, from the hospital, since the hospital didn't have already too many sick people, we took sort of a ride into Berlin in May 7 or 8, approximately, 1945.
We came to Berlin when the Reichstag was burning, and we came to the Kaiser Wilhelm Monument.
We saw a lot of Russians who came there at the same time.
And this was just the good feeling that we had some accomplishment, some input into the, the destruction or the defeat of the German army.
In Biesenthal, that city, we were there another-- probably May until the end of June.
In June, our hospital was evacuated, sort of to say.
The sick people were not there anymore, but we went back on a territory, which was, before the war, a German city.
It was called Rokitnica.
And our hospital was told to establish there.
We were already in buildings.
At that time, of course, I had a room with a shower.
And at that time, I started to look for my brother.
And through every sick person who was coming, first I was looking if it's not my brother himself because I looked at the faces.
And then whoever came, I was finding out, and somehow-- I don't remember the details how I found out to where my brother was.
He was in the Second Army, and he was southern-- that he was sent to the southern part, through Breslau into, I think, Dresden.
And a few days before the war ended, he was injured.
And even though they gave him the first aid, and they-- he was very lucky because he was injured in the spine, but luckily, his spinal cord was not touched.
He was injured below the spinal cord, in what is called coccyx, the, the anatomical name.
So he had a bone injury-- muscular and bone injury, but no-- no-- he was left-- he wasn't left paralyzed.
When, when I found out where my brother is, I wrote to him, and I asked him to come to the, to my hospital.

At the same time, in Rokitnica already, somehow, either because of a general depression of what I went through, or because I had absolutely no interest to go out on the street to buy clothes, my main aim was to be in the hospital.
And this, I devoted my entire time in my life to helping the injured and the people.
And there, somehow the gratitude from the, from the soldiers-- some came and wanted to give me gold bracelet with a ring that they maybe took somewhere from a German.
I had absolutely no sense of value.
I didn't-- it didn't interest me, gold or jewelry.
A dress from one, I said, if you can bring me a dress, because I'm sick and tired of wearing that uniform.
And the white uniforms are the military.
So anyhow, this is how I was in the army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until-- my brother came.
Somehow he came, he also took a furlough for two weeks.
And he came first to my hospital.
So I was asking all the doctors at my hospital they should examine him.
And they examined, and they cleaned up his-- his bone was always oozing with some pus because the outer-- it, it was not a penetrating wound, but it was pretty deep.
So they cleaned out, and he said that it will take time until it will stop oozing the pus.
But if he wants to, he can remain in the hospital in some kind of capacity.
At the same time, Leo Finver took a furlough from his unit, which was also not far away.
But I didn't know where, where they were.
Because, from [NON-ENGLISH], the war was going on, and we were not in contact with each other.
One didn't even have in mind to write to-- and maybe the mail was not delivered.
I'm not very sure.
But the fact is that Leo came.
He took a furlough.
Leo went to our town, to Lesko.
And he went to the next town, Sanok.
In Sanok, he saw Irene [NON-ENGLISH] who survived.
This is the nurse who was hidden by Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I say Irene [NON-ENGLISH] because she married him later on.
And she said, oh, Rita is alive?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
She knew that I was alive.
This I want to go back.
When our town was just like-- Leo wrote to Lesko, to the postmaster, I wrote to Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I, I took a chance.
I thought, he's not a Jew.
He'll be alive.
When I wrote to Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH], Irene was a survivor.
She was hidden by him.
So she wrote back to me on the military number, on the field post number.
And she wrote to me, look, I want to know if my mother and my sisters are still alive.
After all, they were sent away by Stalin deep into Russia.
This is their number.
This is their address.
I cannot drive to them, because there's no post, but if you can.
So I was writing to Russia, to Irene's mother and three sisters, and they were writing to me.
And I was sending their letter to Irene, and this is how we kept in touch.
And Irene found out that her mother and three sisters are alive in Russia.
This, this I thought was of-- of some value-- of some-- to show how the contact of some people, a little thing can help somebody.
So because of that, not only because of the sentiment and the friendship from before the war, Irene told to Leo, if you are going to Rita, please take me along.
So he in Sanok told her, look, the train-- some trains are running, some are not.
But since I'm a soldier, wherever I will go, you come along with me.
And he somehow managed with a little bit of vodka.
You know, a man has got more tricks of the trade.
He came to Rokitnica, to the hospital with Irene.
And Irene said to me, listen, I, I survived only because Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] is an unusual human being.
Otherwise, there are no Jews in Lesko.
The Jewish streets, the Jewish housing is all destroyed.
Only the good houses, which were made from brick-- ours was made from brick, but it was very close to the synagogue, so they destroyed it, too.
She told me about my, my parents, that until they took them to Zaslaw, to the ghetto, which they made a regional ghetto-- 25,000 Jews from Sanok, from Wujskie, from Lesko, were in Zaslaw.
They-- until then, my mother was selling my clothes that I left.
I brought good clothes from Katowice, whatever was not taken away from me.
That they sold my clothes to have to buy a piece of bread.
And as far as she knows, my mother, father, my brother David, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] were taken to Zaslaw.
From Zaslaw, when there was the deportation from Zaslaw, the people went  to Belzec.
And there was a total killing and annihilation of all the Jews who came there.
So Irene's words were, you see this ground you're walking on?
The Polish ground, it's saturated with Jewish blood.
Your input into the war, your function is finished.
Have no sentiment for no pause.
Have no scruples.
Do whatever is good to you, for you.
And Leo at that time said he could go to Cracow and get documents.
And my brother also said, if you get the documents that we can run away together, then I will go.
If not, my brother didn't want to be in the hospital.
He said, if not, I would rather go to my unit.
In spite of everything, he was on a tank, and the unit-- seven, eight people on the tank-- were like one family.
And he said, if I, I'm not going to be in, in the kitchen working or in, in the laundry room, I would rather go where I-- so we decided, and I decided, even though I left everything.
I left my documents.
I left, perhaps, very good references as to my work.
But we ran away the 20th of August.
We came to Katowice.
We took off our uniforms.
I was left in a dress and maybe a slip and nothing really much-- two blouses.
And my brother the same.
And we went on a train.
The papers that Leo brought along, we didn't even have time to look what names we have.
They, they were bought in Cracow from a Czechoslovakian consul through a, through his men who works there, which was getting-- you know, was bribed.
When we came on the train from Katowice to the first border, which was not too far away, it was dark.
There was no light.
Absolutely no light, because it was still, still like the wartime of, of the blackouts.
And we didn't know even what our names were.
And we came to the first post, and the Polish-- the Polish guard, the, the guard at the border, took us all in.
And they took away a lot of things from Leo.
I think he had a few-- $5 and a tube of, of toothpaste.
I had quite a few of my military, military pictures.
I was frantic.
I was petrified.
But somehow they were looking for smugglers.
And they saw that we are not smugglers, so they let us go.
Because of that, they said, go back on the other side.
They thought that we are some kind of black marketeering.
But at that time, through a, a roundabout way-- I don't even remember if we were in Bratislava first or how we came until we came to Prague, where there was the Jewish committee and the Briha.
Now, it's really the most admirable thing how the Briha was working.
They were registering everybody.
They were giving us moral support, that they will try to give us papers to go through from one, through the boundaries.
And from Prague, again, we went wherever they send us.
And they told us not to talk Russian and not to talk Polish, that we are, like, Greek, Greek citizens or we are going back.
We're going back to our home to look for our homes.
And at the same time in Prague, two of our friends met us.
They also did not survive in-- under the Germans.
One was also in Russia, and he was a dental technician.
He was in the Russian army, but because he saw us, he also decided to undress.
He's still alive.
He's in Israel.
He joined us.
The Briha brought us until we came on the Austrian territory.
I wouldn't call all the stops, but it took a while until they brought us to Bad Gastein.
Bad Gastein was a beautiful resort place with beautiful hotels.
The hotels were empty because the German military was apparently recuperating there.
And after-- they were empty when we came.
So the American occupation, the American Army, gave us-- we were probably the first one who came.
This was August, probably the end of September.
We came to Bad Gastein, and they told us you could go in any room you want to.
And we went into a hotel, [NON-ENGLISH], which the room was a little bit bigger than that.
And it had four bunk beds.
And in that room, there were seven boys, and I was the only girl.
And we did not cook there because the Red Cross gave packages to the kitchen.
We went down to the kitchen.
Because of that, if somebody was working, they did get, later on, a little bit-- 600 gram bread.
This was later when the Austrian government already was making the, you know, the coupons, for bread coupons, whoever worked.
I immediately, with a doctor who is still alive-- he's in Binghamton, New York-- started to clean the room and putting together shelves.
We should be able to take in patients.
From United Nations world organization, a nurse-- a Belgian-born nurse, who was an extreme human being with a character that you don't have people like that.
She was probably-- she told me she was in the Belgian resistance, hating the Germans.

She came and she provided us-- together with a doctor from England-- provided medication, basic medication, provided dressings, provided some instruments, a small sterilization where you autoclave the thing.
And we opened the clinic.
And people from Mauthausen and from Ebensee and from the surrounding areas were coming to the clinic to get first aid or whatever need they-- whatever they needed.
The, the type of, of-- if somebody needed, let's say, more bread, through the doctor, sometimes you got it, sometimes not.
Sometimes a little bit more milk for the children.
Until later on, beginning of 1946, a hospital opened in Bad Gastein.
In Bad Gastein, the hospital, they took over an Austrian hospital, but it was predominantly for the local people but predominantly for, for the survivors.
And the hospital did an awful lot of good.
And the Jewish organization ORT has given there courses, nursing courses, in order to have Jewish nurses.
And the course was very, very congested.
And every doctor was so devoted, even though they were not professors by-- they were not teachers.
But they gave an excellent program.
And after six months, a lot of girls-- for me, it was more upgrading course or a refreshing course.
For others, it was a basic, basic course.
When I came to United States, the ORT, the certificate didn't mean anything.
United States, you need it from accredited school documents.
But a lot of other girls who did get nursing school in Hofgastein went in 1948 to Israel.
And during the Israeli Independence War, they were very helpful.
And they entire life worked in Israel because I was in touch with quite a few of them.
Hofgastein was near Bad Gastein.
Hofgastein was, was nearby.
It was maybe 10 kilometers away.
The hospital was there.
Bad Gastein was more of a resort-type of place.
We were there in 1946, when Leo, through the Briha-- one of our friends who brought us through Czechoslovakia, and, and through Vienna, he brought us to Salzburg and to Bad Gastein.
He was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was the guide of the Briha.
And he was very friendly.
We became very friendly.
So when the trains where established, and whenever the big transfers from Poland came, and they came to Salzburg-- Salzburg was a transit place, and the Briha took them there.
From there, some of them went through Italy, to Italy, some to Palestine.
Some went to Germany to look for some friends.
So Leo frequently was going to Salzburg, and he was looking always if somebody registered from our town.
It so happened that it was not difficult, because some girls already was with somebody, somebody else, and they got married, so they wrote their married name.
But if they mentioned that I was born in Lesko-- from there, Leo went a few times with the Briha group to Germany.
And he got in touch-- he found my cousin, Rachel.
My cousin Rachel was working in a little children's nursery there.
Or in a children's-- in a little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, in-- with children-aged kids.
She was working there.
So he saw her, and he asked her does she want to come to us, to Bad Gastein?
Does she want that we are already in contact with some relatives that we found.
I knew the address, and we, we wrote to our relatives, to the United States.
They send us papers.
So she said, I don't care to go to the United States.
I will remain here, and from here, I'm going to Israel.
And she is still alive.
This is Rachel, who was with me in Russia.
Thanks to her-- she was feeding me with a little bit of milk, with a little bit of water.
Otherwise I would have died there without medication, in that [NON-ENGLISH] in the Muslim-- I told you.
So anyhow, she's alive.
And she has a beautiful family.
And they're very active, and they are patriots of Israel.
So Rachel didn't want to, to go with us.
We received the papers through, through-- my uncle wrote to an American soldier.
The soldier gave it to us, because the post office was not even established.
Leo also, with his family that was in the United States-- his father's two sisters were in the United States.
And my mother's two, two, two brothers and a sister were in United States from 1914, from before the first, during the First World War.
So they immediately wrote to the soldiers-- soldiers, this is how we-- they send us papers.
So I registered for the United States.
And Leo-- and then we got married, so he had papers to his relative.
I and my brothers had papers to my relatives.
And this was 1946.
We went through all the preliminary interrogations, through FBI, through CIA.
If we are not whatever, whatever-- I bet if we were German SS, we would have come here faster.
But at that time, we came on a regular quota.
But the quota-- the Jewish, Polish quota was not good.
Never.
The quota from England was very good.
From Germany it was good.
From Belgium it was good.
If somebody registered that he was born in Belgium, he could come faster.
If some-- yeah.
Besides that, children's two parents were on the first, first line, brothers and sisters.
But we, since the uncles hand us papers, we prepared ourselves.
After the first group went to the United States, we prepared ourselves to go next, with the next transport, but it didn't materialize.
I could have used that time to study something, but as it was, I was working in the clinic.
I went to a lab to study lab technician.
I was always learning.
We went to take English courses.
We did not waste the time altogether.
But as far as my diploma, after we came to United States-- I came to United States without documents, as far as nursing documents.
--or is there something--
We arrived here-- we arrived here.
Even coming to the United States wasn't easy.
From Bad Gastein, since this was a, a resort place, the Austrians eventually, where they gained a little bit of more independence, a little-- the voice-- they raised their voices.
They say they want to have Bad Gastein, that, that refugees shouldn't be in Bad Gastein.
And a lot of people-- it was [INAUDIBLE] out, so they took the group from Bad Gastein and made-- and from adjoining camps-- and they made one big camp in what was called Ebelsberg.
I think Ebelsberg.
But immediately, as we came to Ebelsberg-- this was in November, October 1943, beginning of the November.
Immediately as we--
I'm sorry.
I'm sorry.
When we came there, maybe a week later, they told us that the consul is calling us, and we are going to Munich, Germany.
When we came to Munich, Germany, we had to go through, again, all kinds of interrogations, through consuls.
We were staying in Munich, Germany.
And we were in a funkaserne in terrible conditions.
This was November, December 1947.
We were there together, again 10, 12 people were in one huge room.
And the bathroom was in the hallway.
It was a funkaserne from, from, from German soldiers, from before the war.
Barracks.
Not-- yeah, barracks with-- but anyhow, we were there.
Why?
Because this was the very cold winter in-- the end of 1947, beginning of 1948, where the ports were frozen.
There was, I think, a strike of the coal miners at the same time.
There was something that they couldn't send us from the funkaserne to Bremenhaufen to a--
--to the ship.
But eventually, they sent us to, to Bremenhaufen.
And then, in January, I think, the last week in January 1948, beginning 1948, we were put on a ship, which was also a military ship.
And the conditions were not the best.
But we came to the United States in January.
January 29, 1948, we arrived in the States, to New York City.
At the boat, which arrived, Leo's uncle waited at the board-- at the boat.
And one aunt waited.
The other one was ill, and she had to go away, but her husband remained.
And we were forever grateful, because they really needed a winter vacation.
They were always going away for a winter vacation.
As for my uncle, my uncle from Chicago couldn't come.
But he sent a friend of his who lived in-- a childhood friend, and a good friend from-- for all the years-- with $50 for myself, for my brother, and for Leo.
And this should be that we should come.
We should have money for the trip to Chicago.
Since Leo's also uncle was there, and I was married, so Leo's uncle said, let's first come to us.
And he said to my brother also, why do you rush?
You come to us.
You will stay.
This was a Thursday night.
You will stay over Friday, Saturday.
Sunday, I go to join my wife-- which was on vacation in Arkansas, in Hot Springs, where-- she had arthritis.
She said, but meanwhile you come to me.
You will see how we live, and if you want to work for us.
Something like that.
But my brother said immediately, I don't want to go anywhere since my uncle sent me $50.
So I will go to Chicago.
So Leo's uncle took him to Pennsylvania railroad station, and he brought him to the train.
A little bit of English we knew already, but still he was very helpful.
And my brother went immediately to Chicago.
We came Thursday night, and  we slept over in the Bronx in the aunt's apartment.
But Friday, we decided to go to Spring Valley.
That uncle lived in-- Leo's uncle lived in Spring Valley.
And they had a hotel.
And he said right away, if you want to work for us, in the summer, during the holidays, you can always make a few dollars.
So we decided to go first to see how.
And we, ourselves, managed on Friday, with our broken English.
We didn't know how a taxi looked.
We didn't know where the bus terminal-- oh, it's finished.
I'm sorry.
We'll just take a break and get--
Yeah.
Yeah.
A little bit more.
Uh huh.
There won't be too much.
